docs.title: Routing Application
docs.text:
Group/Version routing.eda.nokia.com/v1 Supported OS Nokia SR Linux: 24.10.*, 25.3.*, 25.7.*, 25.10.*, 26.3.* Nokia SR OS: 24.10.r4+, 25.3.r2+, 25.7.*, 25.10.*, 26.3.* Arista EOS: 4.33.2f:beta Cisco NX-OS: 10.5.2:alpha Catalog Nokia/catalog/routing Source Code coming soon

The Routing app is one of the cornerstones of EDA, and of any datacenter network. It contains resources that configure the underlay connectivity of the network that are used to exchange service routes, as well as workflows that provide verification and debugging capabilities.

The application provides the following components:

ResourcesWorkflows
  • DefaultRouter
  • DefaultInterface

  • SystemInterface

  • Drain

  • AttachmentLookup
  • RouteLookup
  • RouteTrace
  • SystemPing

Reachability information in a typical datacenter can be divided into two categories

  • Underlay: reachability information for the network elements in the datacenter
  • Overlay: EVPN routes with reachability information on the hosts that are connected to virtual networking services

This is a very generalizing definition, and is frequently too conservative. For example, one can have an in-band virtual management network for the switches and/or computes, which is subsequently leaked into the default router.

docs.title: Overlay and Underlay concepts
docs.text:
The documentation frequently refers to "underlay routing" and "overlay routing". To explain the difference between the two, we focus on one of the cornerstones of datacenter networks — virtualized services — and explain how underlay and overlay networking works together to enable connectivity between two hosts.

An introduction to virtualized services

Generalization galore

This section contains a lot of generalizations and simplifications, to the point where some of it may be inaccurate in certain scenarios. This section is meant as a very basic introduction into service routing, and should be read as such.

Datacenter networking (and any networking for that matter) is all about providing connectivity for clients. For the sake of this discussion, clients are computers. In reality, clients can be anything from mobile subscribers to firewalls, mainframes, badge readers, barcode scanners, security cameras, and so much more.

In its simplest form, two computers can talk to each other by connecting them with a cable:

This is technically a network with two hosts, but isn't very flexible: what if a third PC is connected? We could connect computer B to computer C, but that too has big limitations: if computer B shuts down, computer A can no longer communicate with computer C:

We need a device that provides point-to-multipoint connectivity: either by replicating every packet to all other clients (hub) or by intelligently passing the packets to the right computer (switch).

We're getting somewhere now! A collection of networking equipment and physical links that enable computers to talk to each other without restrictions is called a broadcast domain or bridged domain, and operates entirely on layer 2 traffic. As our network grows though, we will need to up our security and isolate different groups of clients.

For example, we may want to add a bunch of security cameras that we want to separate from our main computer network. That is where VLANs come into play: VLAN tags are numbers that are added to the ethernet header of a packet, and isolate traffic that is tagged with different VLANs.

Note

There are many more reasons why you want to isolate traffic other than security, for example to limit the amount of broadcast traffic that each connected client receives.

But we have a problem: now that the two broadcast domains are isolated from each other, our computers can't connect to the cameras anymore. To fix this, we need a router that routes connectivity from one IP subnet to another. This is the first time that our networking equipment needs to become aware of the IP addresses that the hosts will use to communicate: previously, the network acted as a mailbox, whereas from now on the network behaves more like a postman.

The network has now grown so much that the clients can no longer all connect to the same network device. The network turns into a proper datacenter.

For reasons beyond the scope of this explanation, exchanging IP reachability information using BGP as a route exchange protocol in this way will not work. We need a protocol that allows broadcast domains to stretch over multiple networking switches.

In the datacenter world, EVPN is most frequently used for the exchange of service routes: it is a very flexible routing protocol that uses transport tunnels as a next hop instead of IP addresses. These transport tunnels are set up between two routers and support traffic from multiple different services, ensuring traffic remains isolated at the remote end of the tunnel.

Transport tunnels

The two most common transport protocols are Multiprotocol Label Switching (MPLS) and Virtual Extensible LAN (VxLAN): MPLS uses labels to establish a tunnel, while VxLAN encapsulates traffic in an IP datagram with the source and destination IP address of network switches. VxLAN is often preferred in the datacenter, among other reasons because it can operate over existing (non-service aware) networks.

docs.title: Overlay routing
docs.text:
When the documentation refers to overlay routing, it is synonymous with data plane traffic within a service: it enables client traffic to go from one point in the datacenter to another, ensuring that the traffic remains isolated to the virtual bridge domain or virtual router. If EVPN is used, these service tunnels are identified by their EVPN Instance ID (EVI) and underlying transport tunnel.

EVPN routes act as traffic rules and are created by networking equipment. They are communicated through the underlay MP-BGP sessions in the DefaultRouter.

docs.title: Underlay routing
docs.text:
When the documentation refers to underlay routing, it is synonymous with control plane traffic in the DefaultRouter of the switches: it enables the network elements to communicate with each other using the BGP, OSPF, or IS-IS routing protocol to find the optimal path through the network. These protocols exchange system IP addresses that will be used to establish the MP-BGP sessions that exchange overlay routes.

Think of the underlay as highways that interconnect cities, and overlay routing as the traffic rules that everyone must follow: these rules determine which packets can take which exits and which lane they must follow once they exit the highway.

docs.title: Attachment Lookup
docs.text:
Workflows → ALAttachment Lookups

The AttachmentLookup workflow searches all selected nodes for an interface configured with a certain IP address. This is useful for debugging purposes, for example when looking for the node with a particular system IP.

Note

This workflow only looks for interfaces that are configured with the IP address. If you want to look for nodes that have a route toward a particular IP address (for example, both sides of a point-to-point link), use the RouteLookup workflow instead.

The workflow input must contain the IP address that a lookup is requested for, and an optional list of nodes to perform the lookup on. If neither the nodeSelectors nor nodes property is specified, the workflow runs on all nodes.

By default, the workflow queries the interfaces of the DefaultRouter. A different router can be specified by using the networkInstance property.

Warning

This workflow does not rely on EDA to do the lookup: it investigates the routing tables on the nodes directly. Currently, only SR Linux nodes are supported. Other operating systems are silently skipped.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: The AttachmentLookup workflow has no dependencies.

docs.title: Referenced resources
docs.text: The AttachmentLookup workflow has no references to any other EDA resources.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
YAMLkubectl 
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: AttachmentLookup
metadata:
  name: attachmentlookup-19021ffa-c19a-4798-b80b-f39cb21a8b77
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 11.0.0.4
  nodes:
    - leaf-1
    - leaf-2
    - spine-1
    - spine-2

cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: AttachmentLookup
metadata:
  name: attachmentlookup-19021ffa-c19a-4798-b80b-f39cb21a8b77
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 11.0.0.4
  nodes:
    - leaf-1
    - leaf-2
    - spine-1
    - spine-2
EOF
Example execution

The workflow correctly identified spine-2 as the owner of IP 11.0.0.4

apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: AttachmentLookup
metadata:
    annotations:
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/id: '29'
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-group: routing.eda.nokia.com
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-kind: AttachmentLookup
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-name: attachmentlookup-b9be2e94-0175-425a-bc60-abfd97cc4af1
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-namespace: eda
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-version: v1
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/state: Completed
        workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/username: admin
    name: attachmentlookup-b9be2e94-0175-425a-bc60-abfd97cc4af1
    namespace: eda
spec:
    address: 11.0.0.4
    nodes:
        - leaf-1
        - leaf-2
        - spine-1
        - spine-2
    status:
        found: true
        results:
            - interface: system0
              networkInstance: default
              node: spine-2
workflowStatus:
    stages:
        - name: Initializing
        state: Completed
        - name: performing-lookup
        state: Completed
state: Completed

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

AttachmentLookup

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

This workflow is used to look up attachments (where an address is attached in the network) on a set of nodes. It takes an address, and an optional list of nodes (or node selectors - a list of label expressions) to perform the lookup on, and returns the matching attachments, including the node, network instance, prefix, interface, and next hop group ID.

  • address* string #

    Address to perform a lookup for. This is a standard IPv4 or IPv6 address, excluding mask or prefix length, e.g. 12.0.0.1.

  • networkInstance string #

    Network Instance is the locally named network instance to use for the lookup. Can be omitted if the default network instance is to be used.

  • nodeSelectors array[string] #

    NodeSelectors is a list of node selectors to execute lookups on. This is a list of label expressions, e.g. ["eda.nokia.com/role=leaf", "eda.nokia.com/region=us-west"].

  • nodes array[string] #

    Nodes is a list of node names to execute lookups on.

STATUS

AttachmentLookupStatus defines the observed state of AttachmentLookup

  • found* boolean #
  • results array[object] #
    • [] object #
      • interface string #
      • networkInstance string #
      • nextHopGroupID integer # format: int64
      • node string #
      • prefix string #

docs.title: Default Interface
docs.text:
Underlay Routing → DIDefault Interfaces

The DefaultInterface resource links an Interface to the DefaultRouter, with the addition of an optional VLAN tag. It can be configured with an IP-Prefix or IPv6 unnumbered, which configures the IP that will be associated with the interface.

Best deployed as part of a Fabric

When possible, we recommend that you deploy this resource through a Fabric which automatically creates two DefaultInterfaces for every inter-switch link: one on each side of the Link.

docs.title: BFD
docs.text:
BFD parameters can be configured on a DefaultInterface. The configured BFD session will monitor the neighboring interface, improving the fault detection time significantly if there is layer-2-only equipment in between the two switches.

Not seeing BFD sessions being established?

BFD requires a protocol to subscribe before a BFD session is created. This could be either a static route, a BGP peer, or OSPF neighbor. For example, a BGPPeer with BFD enabled will only establish a session with its peer if the underlying DefaultInterface has BFD enabled as well, and vice versa.

docs.title: DefaultRouter
docs.text: The DefaultInterface is always connected to a DefaultRouter, which is the abstraction for the routing table that will install the IP subnet configured on the interface. The DefaultRouter must be created before the DefaultInterface can be attached to it.

docs.title: Referenced resources
docs.text: The DefaultInterface has no references to any other EDA resources.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
YAMLkubectl 
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: DefaultInterface
metadata:
  name: interface-leaf-1-ethernet-1-3
  namespace: eda
spec:
  bfd:
    desiredMinTransmitIntMs: 1000
    detectionMultiplier: 3
    enabled: true
    requiredMinEchoReceiveIntMs: 1000
    requiredMinReceiveIntMs: 1000
  defaultRouter: router-leaf-1
  interface: leaf-1-ethernet-1-3
  ipMTU: 8922
  ipv4Addresses:
    - ipPrefix: 12.0.0.8/31

cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: DefaultInterface
metadata:
  name: interface-leaf-1-ethernet-1-3
  namespace: eda
spec:
  bfd:
    desiredMinTransmitIntMs: 1000
    detectionMultiplier: 3
    enabled: true
    requiredMinEchoReceiveIntMs: 1000
    requiredMinReceiveIntMs: 1000
  defaultRouter: router-leaf-1
  interface: leaf-1-ethernet-1-3
  ipMTU: 8922
  ipv4Addresses:
    - ipPrefix: 12.0.0.8/31
EOF

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

DefaultInterface

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

DefaultInterface enables the configuration of default interfaces, including Interface references, DefaultRouter, VLAN IDs, IP MTU settings, and options for IPv4 and IPv6 addresses. It also supports unnumbered interfaces and BFD (Bidirectional Forwarding Detection) configuration.

  • bfd object #

    Enable or disable BFD on this DefaultInterface.

    • desiredMinTransmitIntMs integer #

      The minimum interval in milliseconds between transmission of BFD control packets.

      default: 1000range: 10 to 100000
    • detectionMultiplier integer #

      The number of packets that must be missed to declare this session as down.

      default: 3range: 3 to 20
    • enabled* boolean #

      Enables Biforward Detection.

      default: false
    • requiredMinEchoReceiveIntMs integer #

      The minimum interval between echo packets the local node can receive.

      default: 1000range: 0 to 100000
    • requiredMinReceiveIntMs integer #

      The minimum interval in milliseconds between received BFD control packets that this system should support.

      default: 1000range: 10 to 100000
    • ttl integer #

      Sets custom IP TTL or Hop Limit for multi-hop BFD sessions packets. Not applicable to single-hop BFD sessions.

      range: 2 to 255
  • defaultRouter* string #

    Reference to a DefaultRouter.

  • description string #

    The description of the DefaultInterface.

  • interface* string #

    Reference to an Interface to use for attachment.

  • ipMTU integer #

    Set the IP MTU for the DefaultInterface.

    range: 1280 to 9486
  • ipv4Addresses array[object] #

    List of IPv4 addresses in ip/mask form, e.g., 192.168.0.1/24.

    • [] object #
      • ipPrefix* string #

        IP Address and prefix length in CIDR notation.

      • primary boolean #

        Indicates which address to use as primary.

  • ipv6Addresses array[object] #

    List of IPv6 addresses in ip/mask form, e.g., fc00::1/120.

    • [] object #
      • ipPrefix* string #

        IP Address and prefix length in CIDR notation.

      • primary boolean #

        Indicates which address to use as primary.

  • subinterfaceIndex integer #

    Subinterface index to use with this DefaultInterface. Ignored for platforms that do not support subinterface index

  • unnumbered string #

    Enables the use of unnumbered interfaces on the ISL. For IPv6, no IP address are configured on the sub-interface and only the link local address will be used. If any allocation pool is specified for IPv6 that will take precedence and IPs will be assigned to the interfaces. When using eBGP for an underlay protocol, the DefaultInterfaces which are a part of the ISL will be added to the BGP dynamic neighbor list.

    enum: "IPv6"
  • vlanID integer #

    VLAN to use with this DefaultInterface.

    range: 1 to 4094

STATUS

DefaultInterfaceStatus defines the observed state of DefaultInterface

  • health integer #

    Indicates the health score of the DefaultInterface.

  • healthScoreReason string #

    Indicates the reason for the health score.

  • lastChangeTime string #

    Indicates when this Interface last changed state.

  • operationalState string #

    Indicates the current operational state of the DefaultInterface.

    enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"

docs.title: Default Router
docs.text:
Underlay Routing → DRDefault Routers

The DefaultRouter resource is an abstraction for the main network instance of a router. On most network operating systems this special router has a number of additional features compared to virtual router services:

  • Enables routing protocols like OSPF, IS-IS to operate on (sub)interfaces that are attached to the default router
  • Establishes transport tunnels like VxLAN and MPLS tunnels
  • Originates and advertises MP-BGP service routes like EVPN and BGP-IPVPN routes

Best deployed as part of a Fabric

When possible, we recommend that you deploy this resource through a Fabric which automatically creates a DefaultRouter for every node in the Fabric.

The DefaultRouter resource is the representation of a routing table, which receives IPv4 and IPv6 routes from attached DefaultInterfaces, SystemInterfaces, and BGP neighbors. In addition, it contains the service routes originating from bridged and routed interfaces connected to virtual network services.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
One DefaultRouter resource is linked to a single TopoNode resource, which represents a physical network switch. The DefaultRouter specifies certain global parameters such as:

  • The router ID
  • BGP parameters
  • Route leaking policies
  • Import and export policies that determine which routes are accepted from neighbors and which routes are exported to neighbors

docs.title: BGP
docs.text:
If BGP is used as a protocol to exchange routes from the default router to its neighbors, the BGP section of the DefaultRouter must be enabled and configured.

Most of the properties of the BGP container can be overridden by BGP peers and BGP groups (set of BGP peers that share common parameters). Nevertheless, it is useful to have default values specified in the DefaultRouter resource and to enable all BGP address families that will be exchanged in your network, even if not every BGP peer will be used to exchange all of these families.

Autonomous system number

On Nokia SR OS, the autonomous system number must be present in the DefaultRouter, even if it is overridden in the BGP peer. Without it, BGP sessions will appear as down and no routes will be exchanged.

docs.title: Route leaking
docs.text:
Route leaking is performed when reachability information from a virtual router service needs to be exposed to the default routing table. A use case for route leaking is an in-band management network that needs to be reachable from the default routing table. The inverse is also possible: for example, a default static route towards the internet that is configured in the default router of your network may be exposed to services that have access to the internet.

Often, route leaking is done in both ways simultaneously: public IP addresses are isolated from internal IP addresses through a virtual router service. To enable internet access for those publicly routable IP addresses, a default route towards the internet is leaked from the default router to the virtual router, and the public IP subnet is exposed to the internet by leaking from the virtual router to the default router.

docs.title: TopoNode
docs.text: A DefaultRouter is always linked to exactly one node. The TopoNode resource must be created before the DefaultRouter can be deployed.

docs.title: Policy
docs.text: Import and export routing policies can be configured for route leaking purposes or as global parameters for BGP peering sessions.

docs.title: Keychain
docs.text: BGP keychains contain authentication parameters to secure communication between two BGP peers. If a Keychain is configured in the DefaultRouter, every BGP peer established on a DefaultInterface will use it to authenticate the neighbor unless it is overridden at the group or peer level.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
YAMLkubectl 
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: DefaultRouter
metadata:
  name: router-leaf-1
  namespace: eda
spec:
  bgp:
    autonomousSystem: 65500
    ebgpPreference: 170
    enabled: true
    ibgpPreference: 170
    l2VPNEVPN:
      enabled: true
      interASVPN: false
      multipath:
        allowMultipleAS: true
        maxAllowedPaths: 64
      rapidUpdate: true
      retainAllRoutes: false
    minWaitToAdvertiseSeconds: 0
    rapidWithdrawl: false
    waitForFibInstall: false
  description: 'fabric role: leaf'
  ecmp: 64
  node: leaf-1
  routerID: 11.0.0.2

cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: DefaultRouter
metadata:
  name: router-leaf-1
  namespace: eda
spec:
  bgp:
    autonomousSystem: 65500
    ebgpPreference: 170
    enabled: true
    ibgpPreference: 170
    l2VPNEVPN:
      enabled: true
      interASVPN: false
      multipath:
        allowMultipleAS: true
        maxAllowedPaths: 64
      rapidUpdate: true
      retainAllRoutes: false
    minWaitToAdvertiseSeconds: 0
    rapidWithdrawl: false
    waitForFibInstall: false
  description: 'fabric role: leaf'
  ecmp: 64
  node: leaf-1
  routerID: 11.0.0.2
EOF

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

DefaultRouter

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

DefaultRouter enables the configuration of default routing instances on a specified Node, including options for BGP configuration, import and export policies, and router IDs.

  • bgp object #

    BGP configuration.

    • autonomousSystem integer #

      Autonomous System number for BGP.

      range: 1 to 4294967295
    • ebgpPreference integer #

      Preference to be set for eBGP.

      default: 170range: 1 to 255
    • enabled boolean #

      Enables BGP in the default VRF.

      default: false
    • ibgpPreference integer #

      Preference to be set for iBGP.

      default: 170range: 1 to 255
    • ipv4Unicast object #

      Parameters relating to the IPv4 unicast AFI/SAFI.

      • advertiseIPv6NextHops boolean #

        Enables advertisement of IPv4 Unicast routes with IPv6 next-hops to peers.

      • enabled boolean #

        Enables the IPv4 unicast AFISAFI.

      • multipath object #

        Enable multipath.

        • allowMultipleAS* boolean #

          When set to true, BGP is allowed to build a multipath set using BGP routes with different neighbor AS (most recent AS in the AS_PATH), When set to false, BGP is only allowed to use non-best paths for ECMP if they meet the multipath criteria and they have the same neighbor AS as the best path.

          default: true
        • maxAllowedPaths* integer #

          The maximum number of BGP ECMP next-hops for BGP routes with an NLRI belonging to the address family of this configuration context.

          range: 1 to 256
      • rapidUpdate boolean #

        Enables rapid update.

      • receiveIPv6NextHops boolean #

        Enables the advertisement of the RFC 5549 capability to receive IPv4 routes with IPv6 next-hops.

    • ipv6Unicast object #

      Parameters relating to the IPv6 unicast AFI/SAFI.

      • enabled boolean #

        Enables the IPv6 unicast AFISAFI

      • multipath object #

        Enable multipath

        • allowMultipleAS* boolean #

          When set to true, BGP is allowed to build a multipath set using BGP routes with different neighbor AS (most recent AS in the AS_PATH), When set to false, BGP is only allowed to use non-best paths for ECMP if they meet the multipath criteria and they have the same neighbor AS as the best path.

          default: true
        • maxAllowedPaths* integer #

          The maximum number of BGP ECMP next-hops for BGP routes with an NLRI belonging to the address family of this configuration context.

          range: 1 to 256
      • rapidUpdate boolean #

        Enables rapid update.

    • keychain string #

      Keychain to be used for authentication

    • l2VPNEVPN object #

      Parameters relating to the EVPN AFI/SAFI.

      • advertiseIPv6NextHops boolean #

        Enables advertisement of EVPN routes with IPv6 next-hops to peers.

      • enabled boolean #

        Enables the L2VPN EVPN AFISAFI.

      • interASVPN boolean #

        Enable inter-AS VPN for EVPN.

        default: false
      • multipath object #

        Enable multipath

        • allowMultipleAS* boolean #

          When set to true, BGP is allowed to build a multipath set using BGP routes with different neighbor AS (most recent AS in the AS_PATH), When set to false, BGP is only allowed to use non-best paths for ECMP if they meet the multipath criteria and they have the same neighbor AS as the best path.

          default: true
        • maxAllowedPaths* integer #

          The maximum number of BGP ECMP next-hops for BGP routes with an NLRI belonging to the address family of this configuration context.

          range: 1 to 256
      • rapidUpdate boolean #

        Enables rapid update.

      • retainAllRoutes boolean #

        Retain all routes in RIB-In (even those that are not imported to services).

        default: false
    • minWaitToAdvertiseSeconds integer #

      Minimum wait time in seconds before advertising routes post BGP restart.

      default: 0range: 0 to 3600
    • rapidWithdrawl boolean #

      Enable rapid withdrawal in BGP.

      default: false
    • sendCommunityLarge boolean #

      When false, all large (12 byte) BGP communities from all outbound routes advertised to the peer are stripped.

    • sendCommunityStandard boolean #

      When false, all standard (4 byte) communities from all outbound routes advertised to the peer are stripped.

    • vpnIPv4Unicast object #

      Parameters related to the VPN IPv4 unicast AFI/SAFI.

      • advertiseIPv6NextHops boolean #

        Enables advertisement of VPN IPv4 routes with IPv6 next-hops to peers.

      • enabled* boolean #

        Enables the VPN IPv4 Unicast AFI/SAFI.

      • interASVPN boolean #

        Enables inter-AS VPN.

        default: false
      • rapidUpdate boolean #

        Enables rapid update.

      • receiveIPv6NextHops boolean #

        Enables advertisement of the Extended Next Hop Encoding Capability (RFC 8950) to peers.

      • retainAllRoutes boolean #

        Retain all routes in RIB-In (even those that are not imported to services).

        default: false
    • vpnIPv6Unicast object #

      Parameters related to the VPN IPv6 unicast AFI/SAFI.

      • enabled* boolean #

        Enables the VPN IPv6 Unicast AFI/SAFI.

      • interASVPN boolean #

        Enables inter-AS VPN.

        default: false
      • rapidUpdate boolean #

        Enables rapid update.

      • retainAllRoutes boolean #

        Retain all routes in RIB-In (even those that are not imported to services).

        default: false
    • waitForFibInstall boolean #

      Wait for FIB installation before advertising routes.

      default: false
  • description string #

    Sets the description on the Default router.

  • ecmp integer #

    Set the maximum number of ECMP paths for the DefaultRouter. This is supported only by some platforms, and will be ignored for others.

    range: 1 to 256
  • exportPolicies array[string] #

    Reference to a Policy resource to use when evaluating route exports from the DefaultRouter.

  • importPolicies array[string] #

    Reference to a Policy resource to use when evaluating route imports into the DefaultRouter.

  • node* string #

    Reference to a TopoNode on which to configure the default routing instance.

  • routeLeaking object #

    Route leaking controlled by routing policies in and out of the DefaultRouter.

    • exportPolicy* string #

      Reference to a Policy resource to use when evaluating route exports from the DefaultRouter.

    • importPolicy* string #

      Reference to a Policy resource to use when evaluating route imports into the DefaultRouter.

  • routerID* string #

    Router ID in dotted quad notation.

STATUS

DefaultRouterStatus defines the observed state of DefaultRouter

  • health integer #

    Indicates the health score of the Router.

  • healthScoreReason string #

    Indicates the reason for the health score.

  • lastChangeTime string #

    The time when the state of the resource last changed.

  • operationalState string #

    Operational state of the Router.

    enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"

docs.title: Drain
docs.text:
Maintenance → DDrains

The Drain resource is used to divert traffic away from a certain node. It accomplishes this by installing a routing Policy that artificially extends the AS path of outgoing BGP routes, causing peers to uninstall those routes as they are no longer the most optimal.

Warning

Installing a Drain resource on a DefaultRouter does not stop the advertisement of any routes, and therefore does not drop traffic: if the only route to a particular IP-prefix goes through the drained DefaultRouter, it will still go through that node!

This resource is usually created as part of a workflow, like the DeployImage workflow.

docs.title: DefaultRouter
docs.text: The Drain resource creates and installs a BGP routing Policy which is installed in any selected DefaultRouter. If these DefaultRouter resources are selected using the defaultRouters property, they must exist before the Drain resource is created.

docs.title: Referenced resources
docs.text: The Drain has no references to any other EDA resources.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
YAMLkubectl 
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: Drain
metadata:
  namespace: eda
  name: my-drain
spec:
  enabled: true
  defaultRouters:
    - router-leaf-1
    - router-spine-2

cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: Drain
metadata:
  namespace: eda
  name: my-drain
spec:
  enabled: true
  defaultRouters:
    - router-leaf-1
    - router-spine-2
EOF

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

Drain

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

Drain allows for the controlled disabling or draining of specific DefaultRouters, either by selecting them through labels or directly referencing them, ensuring traffic is safely rerouted or dropped before the routers are decommissioned.

  • defaultRouterSelectors array[string] #

    Selector to use when selecting DefaultRouters to drain.

  • defaultRouters array[string] #

    Reference to DefaultRouters to drain.

  • enabled boolean #

    Enable this Drain.

    default: true

STATUS

DrainStatus defines the observed state of Drain.

  • defaultRouters array[string] #

    List of DefaultRouters this Drain has been applied to

docs.title: Route Lookup
docs.text:
Workflows → RLRoute Lookups

This workflow looks through the routing table of all selected nodes for a route towards the specified IP address. The result is a list of nodes that were searched, including whether a route was found and if so, what the next-hops are for that route.

By default, the workflow performs a lookup in the DefaultRouter. A different router can be specified by using the networkInstance property.

Warning

This workflow does not rely on EDA to do the lookup: it investigates the routing tables on the nodes directly. Currently, only SR Linux nodes are supported. Other operating systems are silently skipped.

docs.title: Resolve property
docs.text: If resolve is set to true, the resolved next-hop for that route will be included in the output.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: The RouteLookup workflow has no dependencies.

docs.title: Referenced resources
docs.text: The RouteLookup workflow has no references to any other EDA resources.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
YAMLkubectl 
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: RouteLookup
metadata:
  name: routelookup-0f719699-5958-488e-a417-5149a200898c
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 10.30.1.24
  networkInstance: inventory-router
  resolve: true

cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: RouteLookup
metadata:
  name: routelookup-0f719699-5958-488e-a417-5149a200898c
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 10.30.1.24
  networkInstance: inventory-router
  resolve: true
EOF
Example execution

The workflow correctly discovered 3 nodes that have a route towards IP 10.30.1.24 in the inventory-router network instance:

  • Node leaf12 has an indirect route and will send these packets to leaf11
  • Node leaf13 has an indirect route and will send these packets to leaf11
  • Node leaf11 has a direct route and will send these packets out over sub-interface ethernet-1/1.1311

The two spine nodes do not terminate the inventory-router service and do not have a route for this IP.

apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: RouteLookup
metadata:
  annotations:
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/id: '1'
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-group: routing.eda.nokia.com
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-kind: RouteLookup
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-name: routelookup-2d5801bd-a80e-4f64-8fe9-fca57c3a6a73
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-namespace: eda
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-version: v1
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/state: Completed
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/username: admin
  name: routelookup-2d5801bd-a80e-4f64-8fe9-fca57c3a6a73
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 10.30.1.24
  networkInstance: inventory-router
  resolve: false
status:
  found: true
  nodesWithRoute: 3
  results:
    - found: false
      networkInstance: inventory-router
      node: g99-spine11
    - found: false
      networkInstance: inventory-router
      node: g99-spine12
    - found: true
      networkInstance: inventory-router
      nextHopGroupID: 8605221109
      nextHops:
        - interfaces:
            - localAddress: 10.30.1.1/24
              name: ethernet-1/1.1311
              peerNode: g99-leaf11
          ipAddress: 10.30.1.1
          nextHopID: 8605221096
          type: Direct
      node: g99-leaf11
      rawOutput: |-
        /network-instance[name=inventory-router]/route-table:
            10.30.1.0/24
      route: 10.30.1.0/24
    - found: true
      networkInstance: inventory-router
      nextHopGroupID: 8605387614
      nextHops:
        - ipAddress: 10.46.99.31
          nextHopID: 8605387596
          type: Indirect
      node: g99-leaf13
      rawOutput: |-
        /network-instance[name=inventory-router]/route-table:
            10.30.1.0/24
      route: 10.30.1.0/24
    - found: true
      networkInstance: inventory-router
      nextHopGroupID: 8605208653
      nextHops:
        - ipAddress: 10.46.99.31
          nextHopID: 8605208636
          type: Indirect
      node: g99-leaf12
      rawOutput: |-
        /network-instance[name=inventory-router]/route-table:
            10.30.1.0/24
      route: 10.30.1.0/24
workflowStatus:
  stages:
    - name: Initializing
      state: Completed
    - name: performing-lookup
      state: Completed
  state: Completed

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

RouteLookup

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

This workflow is used to look up routes on a set of nodes. It takes an address, and an optional list of nodes (or node selectors - a list of label expressions) to perform the lookup on, and returns the matching route, and the set of egress interfaces that would be used to reach it.

  • address* string #

    Address to perform a lookup for. This is a standard IPv4 or IPv6 address, excluding mask or prefix length, e.g. 12.0.0.1.

  • networkInstance string #

    Network Instance is the locally named network instance to use for the lookup. Can be omitted if the default network instance is to be used.

  • nodeSelectors array[string] #

    Node Selectors is a list of node label selectors to execute lookups on. This is a list of label expressions, e.g. ["eda.nokia.com/role=leaf", "eda.nokia.com/region=us-west"].

  • nodes array[string] #

    Nodes is a list of node names to execute lookups on.

  • resolve* boolean #

    Resolve indicates whether indirect next hops should be resolved.

    default: true

STATUS

RouteLookupStatus defines the observed state of RouteLookup

  • found* boolean #
  • nodesWithRoute integer #
  • results array[object] #
    • [] object #
      • error string #
      • found* boolean #
      • networkInstance string #
      • nextHopGroupID integer # format: int64
      • nextHops array[object] #
        • [] object #
          • indirect object #
            • ipPrefix string #
            • nextHopGroupID integer # format: int64
            • resolved boolean #
            • type string #
          • interfaces array[object] #
            • [] object #
              • localAddress string #

                Local IP address of the egress interface

              • name* string #

                Name of the egress interface.

              • peerNode string #

                The node this interface is connected to.

          • ipAddress string #
          • nextHopID integer # format: int64
          • type string #
      • node string #
      • rawOutput string #
      • route string #
  • totalNodes integer #

docs.title: Route Trace
docs.text:
Workflows → RTRoute Traces

The RouteTrace workflow analyzes the route a packet would take from a source to a destination, by looking at the routing table of the source and all nodes on the (ECMP) path.

The input parameters of the workflow are:

  • The destination IP address of the packet
  • The node that the packet starts from
  • The network instance that the lookups are performed in

The workflow result is a list of possible links that the packet may use to get to the destination.

Not a ping

The workflow does not send any traffic: it analyzes the routing table directly, and will create one result row per ECMP path segment.

By default, the workflow uses the route table of the DefaultRouter. A different router can be specified by using the networkInstance property.

Warning

This workflow does not rely on EDA to do the lookup: it investigates the routing tables on the nodes directly. Currently, only SR Linux nodes are supported. Other operating systems are silently skipped.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: The RouteTrace workflow has no dependencies.

docs.title: Referenced resources
docs.text: The RouteTrace workflow has no references to any other EDA resources.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
YAMLkubectl 
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: RouteTrace
metadata:
  name: routetrace-83baa037-937d-4acf-bcb2-03981a3820c6
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 10.46.99.33
  node: leaf11

cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: RouteTrace
metadata:
  name: routetrace-83baa037-937d-4acf-bcb2-03981a3820c6
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 10.46.99.33
  node: leaf11
EOF
Example execution

The workflow correctly discovered 4 links that the packets going from leaf11 to leaf13 may encounter:

  • Two ECMP paths on leaf11
    • One via spine11
    • One via spine12
  • One path from spine11 to leaf13 (the destination)
  • One path from spine12 to leaf13 (the destination)
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: RouteTrace
metadata:
  annotations:
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/id: '3'
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-group: routing.eda.nokia.com
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-kind: RouteTrace
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-name: routetrace-12670557-e0a3-49d3-ba14-264de513a878
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-namespace: eda
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/root-workflow-version: v1
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/state: Completed
    workflows.core.eda.nokia.com/username: admin
  name: routetrace-12670557-e0a3-49d3-ba14-264de513a878
  namespace: eda
spec:
  address: 10.46.99.33
  node: leaf11
status:
  links:
    - fromNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/31
        localAddress: fe80::18cc:10ff:feff:1f
        node: leaf11
      toNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/1
        localAddress: fe80::1892:22ff:feff:1
        node: spine11
    - fromNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/32
        localAddress: fe80::18cc:10ff:feff:20
        node: leaf11
      toNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/1
        localAddress: fe80::1817:23ff:feff:1
        node: spine12
    - fromNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/3
        localAddress: fe80::1892:22ff:feff:3
        node: spine11
      toNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/31
        localAddress: fe80::1814:12ff:feff:1f
        node: leaf13
    - fromNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/3
        localAddress: fe80::1817:23ff:feff:3
        node: spine12
      toNode:
        interface: ethernet-1/32
        localAddress: fe80::1814:12ff:feff:20
        node: leaf13
    - fromNode:
        node: leaf13
      toNode:
        destination: true
workflowStatus:
  stages:
    - name: Initializing
      state: Completed
    - name: performing-trace
      state: Completed
  state: Completed

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

RouteTrace

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

RouteTraceSpec defines the desired state of RouteTrace

  • address* string #

    This is a standard IPv4 or IPv6 address, excluding mask or prefix length, e.g. 12.0.0.1.

  • networkInstance string #

    Network Instance is the locally named network instance to use for the lookup. Can be omitted if the default network instance is to be used.

  • node* string #

    Node is the name of the node to execute lookups on.

STATUS

RouteTraceStatus defines the observed state of RouteTrace

  • links* array[object] #
    • [] object #
      • fromNode* object #
        • destination boolean #

          Extract indicate the from node owns the address.

        • direct boolean #

          Direct indicates that the host is directly connected to the node.

        • error string #

          If the trace is not successful, this field will contain an error message on the 'to' node.

        • interface string #

          Interface is the name of the interface on the node that the trace is either entering or exiting.

        • localAddress string #

          LocalAddress is the local address of the endpoint (e.g. the nexthop address on the 'to' node).

        • node string #

          Node is the name of the node where the endpoint is located.

      • toNode* object #
        • destination boolean #

          Extract indicate the from node owns the address.

        • direct boolean #

          Direct indicates that the host is directly connected to the node.

        • error string #

          If the trace is not successful, this field will contain an error message on the 'to' node.

        • interface string #

          Interface is the name of the interface on the node that the trace is either entering or exiting.

        • localAddress string #

          LocalAddress is the local address of the endpoint (e.g. the nexthop address on the 'to' node).

        • node string #

          Node is the name of the node where the endpoint is located.

docs.title: System Interface
docs.text:
Underlay Routing → SISystem Interfaces

The system interface is also known as the router ID, and is a special loopback address in the DefaultRouter used as a unique identifier for the node in the network. The SystemInterface resource is also typically used as the source IP address for setting up MP-BGP sessions that exchange overlay routes.

Best deployed as part of a Fabric

When possible, we recommend that you deploy this resource through a Fabric which automatically creates a SystemInterface for every node in the Fabric.

docs.title: BFD
docs.text:
BFD parameters can be configured on a SystemInterface. The configured BFD session will monitor a remote IP address, improving the fault detection time significantly.

Not seeing BFD sessions being established?

BFD requires a protocol to subscribe before a BFD session is created. This could be either a static route or a BGP peer. For example, a BGPPeer with BFD enabled will only establish a BFD session with its peer if the underlying SystemInterface has BFD enabled as well, and vice versa.

docs.title: DefaultRouter
docs.text: The SystemInterface is always connected to a DefaultRouter, which is the abstraction for the routing table that will install the IP subnet configured on the interface. The DefaultRouter must be created before the SystemInterface can be attached to it.

docs.title: Referenced resources
docs.text: The SystemInterface has no references to any other EDA resources.

docs.title: Examples
docs.text:
YAMLkubectl 
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: SystemInterface
metadata:
  labels:
    eda.nokia.com/source: derived
    fabric: my-fabric
  name: spine-1-system0
  namespace: eda
spec:
  defaultRouter: router-spine-1
  ipv4Address: 11.0.0.3/32

cat << 'EOF' | kubectl apply -f -
apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: SystemInterface
metadata:
  labels:
    eda.nokia.com/source: derived
    fabric: my-fabric
  name: spine-1-system0
  namespace: eda
spec:
  defaultRouter: router-spine-1
  ipv4Address: 11.0.0.3/32
EOF

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

SystemInterface

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

SystemInterfaceSpec defines the desired state of SystemInterface

  • bfd object #

    Enable or disable BFD on this SystemInterface.

    • desiredMinTransmitIntMs integer #

      The minimum interval in milliseconds between transmission of BFD control packets.

      default: 1000range: 10 to 100000
    • detectionMultiplier integer #

      The number of packets that must be missed to declare this session as down.

      default: 3range: 3 to 20
    • enabled* boolean #

      Enables Biforward Detection.

      default: false
    • requiredMinEchoReceiveIntMs integer #

      The minimum interval between echo packets the local node can receive.

      default: 1000range: 0 to 100000
    • requiredMinReceiveIntMs integer #

      The minimum interval in milliseconds between received BFD control packets that this system should support.

      default: 1000range: 10 to 100000
    • ttl integer #

      Sets custom IP TTL or Hop Limit for multi-hop BFD sessions packets. Not applicable to single-hop BFD sessions.

      range: 2 to 255
  • defaultRouter* string #

    Reference to a DefaultRouter.

  • description string #

    The description of the SystemInterface.

  • ipv4Address string #

    IPv4 address in ip/mask form, e.g., 192.168.0.1/32.

  • ipv6Address string #

    IPv6 address in ip/mask form, e.g., fc00::1/128.

STATUS

SystemInterfaceStatus defines the observed state of SystemInterface

  • health integer #

    Indicates the health score of the SystemInterface.

  • healthScoreReason string #

    Indicates the reason for the health score.

  • lastChangeTime string #

    Indicates when this Interface last changed state.

  • operationalState string #

    Indicates the current operational state of the SystemInterface.

    enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"

docs.title: System Ping
docs.text:
Workflows → SPSystem Pings

The SystemPing workflow is used to verify reachability between the nodes via the DefaultRouter. It works by selecting either nodes or SystemInterface resources directly, and pinging between each pair of system IP addresses.

Info

If the nodes or nodeSelectors properties are used, the workflow will look for SystemInterface resources configured on those nodes. If no SystemInterfaces were detected, the node will be ignored.

Pings are done intelligently, meaning that if the workflow already pinged leaf2 from leaf1, there will not be a ping to leaf1 from leaf2.

The workflow result contains an entry for each unique pair of system IP addresses, and the result of the ping between the two including minimum, maximum, and average latency.

docs.title: Dependencies
docs.text: The SystemPing workflow has no dependencies.

docs.title: SystemInterface
docs.text: If the workflow is created for a set of manually selected system interfaces, these resources must be created first.

apiVersion: routing.eda.nokia.com/v1
kind: RouteLookup
metadata:
  name: routelookup-a338a899-0042-4432-b7b1-71e98bd478bf
  namespace: eda
spec:
  resolve: false

  networkInstance: inventory-router

kind: RouteLookup
metadata:

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

SystemPing

routing.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

SystemPing allows performing ping between systems to their system interfaces.

  • addressFamily string #

    AddressFamily is the address family to use for the ping. It can be one of the following values: - "Ipv4": Use Ipv4 addresses for the ping. - "Ipv6": Use Ipv6 addresses for the ping. - "DualStack": Use both Ipv4 and Ipv6 addresses for the ping.

    default: "DualStack"enum: "IPv4", "IPv6", "DualStack"
  • count integer #

    Count is the number of pings to send.

    default: 1
  • nodeSelectors array[string] #

    List of node selectors to select nodes whose system interfaces will be pinged. This matches labels on TopoNode resources. If no nodes and no node selectors and no systemInterfaces as well no systemInterface selectors are specified, then all nodes in the given namespace will be selected. This is a list of label expressions, e.g. ["eda.nokia.com/role=leaf"].

  • nodes array[string] #

    List of nodes whose system interfaces will be pinged. This matches names of TopoNode resources.

  • systemInterfaceSelectors array[string] #

    SystemInterface Selectors is a list of selectors to execute System pings for. This is a list of label expressions, e.g. ["eda.nokia.com/role=leaf", "eda.nokia.com/region=us-west"].

  • systemInterfaces array[string] #

    SystemInterfaces is a list of named SystemInterface resources to execute System pings for.

  • timeoutSeconds integer #

    TimeoutSeconds is the timeout for the ping in seconds.

    default: 5

STATUS

The result of the System ping

  • details array[object] #

    Details contains the results of the pings performed for each SystemInterface. Each entry in the list corresponds to a SystemInterface that was pinged.

    • [] object #
      • details object #

        Details of the ping result, if available.

        • averageTimeNanoseconds integer #

          Average time taken for a ping reply.

          format: int64
        • maxTimeNanoseconds integer #

          Maximum time taken for a ping reply.

          format: int64
        • minTimeNanoseconds integer #

          Minimum time taken for a ping reply.

          format: int64
        • received* integer #

          Number of pings received.

        • sent* integer #

          Number of pings sent.

        • stdDevNanoseconds integer #

          Standard deviation of time taken for all pings.

          format: int64
        • totalTimeNanoseconds integer #

          Total time taken for all pings.

          format: int64
      • error string #

        Error message, if the ping failed.

      • name string #

        Name of the ping result.

      • success* boolean #

        Indicates if the ping was successful.

  • result string #

    Result is the overall result of the ping operation. It can be one of the following values: - "Success": All pings were successful. - "Failed": No pings were successful. - "PartialSuccess": Some pings were successful, but not all.

    enum: "Success", "Failed", "PartialSuccess"

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

Policy

routingpolicies.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

Policy defines a set of rules and actions to manage network traffic or routing behavior, with statements that include matching conditions and actions, such as accepting or rejecting routes, or modifying route attributes like BGP parameters.

  • configuredName string #

    The name of the policy to configure on the device.

  • defaultAction object #

    The default action to apply if no other actions are defined.

    • bgp object #

      Actions related to the BGP protocol.

      • asPathPrepend object #

        AS number to prepend to the AS Path attributes.

        • asn* string #

          AS number to prepend to the AS Path attributes. Can be an integer value or the keyword `Auto`.

        • count integer #

          Number of times to prepend the AS number.

          format: int32range: 1 to 50
      • asPathRemove boolean #

        Clear the AS path to make it empty.

      • asPathReplace array[integer] #

        Replace the existing AS path with a new AS_SEQUENCE containing the listed AS numbers.

      • communities object #

        Modify BGP communities associated with the route using hybrid Community Sets.

        • addSets array[string] #

          List of community sets to add to the route.

        • removeSets array[string] #

          List of community sets to remove from the route.

        • replaceSets array[string] #

          List of community sets to replace the existing communities with. Cannot be combined with Add or Remove.

      • med object #

        Set a new MED value.

        • numericalValue integer #

          Fixed numerical value to set or add/subtract.

          range: 0 to 4294967295
        • operation string #

          The operation to perform on the MED value.

          enum: "Set", "Add", "Subtract"
        • valueType string #

          Use a fixed value or an IGP metric to adjust the MED.

          enum: "Fixed", "IGP"
      • nextHop object #

        Override the BGP next-hop attribute.

        • address string #

          IP Address of the next hop. Only valid when NextHopType is set to FixedIP.

        • type string #

          Set next-hop to either a fixed IP address or to a local IP address of the device using a `Self` or `PeerIP` keyword.

          enum: "FixedIP", "Self", "PeerIP"
      • setLocalPreference integer #

        Set a new LOCAL_PREF value for matching BGP routes.

        format: int32range: 0 to 4294967295
      • setOrigin string #

        Set a new ORIGIN attribute for matching BGP routes.

        enum: "egp", "igp", "incomplete"
    • policyResult string #

      Final disposition for the route.

      enum: "Accept", "Reject", "NextPolicy", "NextStatement"
    • tags object #

      Manipulate internal route tags associated with the route.

      • applyTagSet string #

        Add tags to the route from the referenced Tag Set.

  • statements array[object] #

    List of policy statements.

    • [] object #
      • action object #

        Actions for routes that match the policy statement.

        • bgp object #

          Actions related to the BGP protocol.

          • asPathPrepend object #

            AS number to prepend to the AS Path attributes.

            • asn* string #

              AS number to prepend to the AS Path attributes. Can be an integer value or the keyword `Auto`.

            • count integer #

              Number of times to prepend the AS number.

              format: int32range: 1 to 50
          • asPathRemove boolean #

            Clear the AS path to make it empty.

          • asPathReplace array[integer] #

            Replace the existing AS path with a new AS_SEQUENCE containing the listed AS numbers.

          • communities object #

            Modify BGP communities associated with the route using hybrid Community Sets.

            • addSets array[string] #

              List of community sets to add to the route.

            • removeSets array[string] #

              List of community sets to remove from the route.

            • replaceSets array[string] #

              List of community sets to replace the existing communities with. Cannot be combined with Add or Remove.

          • med object #

            Set a new MED value.

            • numericalValue integer #

              Fixed numerical value to set or add/subtract.

              range: 0 to 4294967295
            • operation string #

              The operation to perform on the MED value.

              enum: "Set", "Add", "Subtract"
            • valueType string #

              Use a fixed value or an IGP metric to adjust the MED.

              enum: "Fixed", "IGP"
          • nextHop object #

            Override the BGP next-hop attribute.

            • address string #

              IP Address of the next hop. Only valid when NextHopType is set to FixedIP.

            • type string #

              Set next-hop to either a fixed IP address or to a local IP address of the device using a `Self` or `PeerIP` keyword.

              enum: "FixedIP", "Self", "PeerIP"
          • setLocalPreference integer #

            Set a new LOCAL_PREF value for matching BGP routes.

            format: int32range: 0 to 4294967295
          • setOrigin string #

            Set a new ORIGIN attribute for matching BGP routes.

            enum: "egp", "igp", "incomplete"
        • policyResult string #

          Final disposition for the route.

          enum: "Accept", "Reject", "NextPolicy", "NextStatement"
        • tags object #

          Manipulate internal route tags associated with the route.

          • applyTagSet string #

            Add tags to the route from the referenced Tag Set.

      • match object #

        Match conditions of the policy statement.

        • bgp object #

          Configuration for BGP-specific policy match criteria.

          • asPathMatch object #

            AS Path match criteria.

            • asPathExpression string #

              A singular regular expression string to match against AS_PATH objects. Mutually exclusive with the ASPathSet reference.

            • asPathSet string #

              Reference to an ASPathSet resource. Mutually exclusive with the ASPathExpression.

            • matchSetOptions string #

              The matching criteria that applies to the members in the referenced set.

              enum: "Any", "All", "Invert"
          • communitySet string #

            Match conditions for BGP communities.

          • evpnRouteTypes array[integer] #

            Match conditions for EVPN route types.

        • families array[string] #

          Address families that the route belongs to.

        • prefixSet string #

          Reference to a PrefixSet resource.

        • protocol string #

          The route protocol type to match.

          enum: "Aggregate", "ARP_ND", "BGP", +12 more
        • tags object #

          Match based on the internal route tags associated with the route.

          • tagSet string #

            Reference to a TagSet resource.

      • name* string #

        Name of the policy statement.

STATUS

PolicyStatus defines the observed state of Policy.

PrefixSet

routingpolicies.eda.nokia.com / v1

Expand All

SPEC

PrefixSet defines a collection of IP prefixes, which may include specific CIDR blocks or a range of prefixes. This set is typically used for matching routes or implementing routing policies.

  • configuredName string #

    The name of the prefixset to configure on the device.

  • prefixes* array[object] #

    List of IPv4 or IPv6 prefixes in CIDR notation.

    • [] object #
      • endRange integer #

        The end range when using a range to match prefixes.

        range: 0 to 128
      • exact boolean #

        Indicates if it is an exact match. Ignores the StartRange and EndRange if this param is set.

      • prefix* string #

        The IPv4 or IPv6 prefix in CIDR notation with mask.

      • startRange integer #

        If specifying a range, this is the start of the range.

        range: 0 to 128

STATUS

PrefixSetStatus defines the observed state of PrefixSet.

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition

To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

BridgeDomain

services.eda.nokia.com / v2

Expand All

SPEC

The BridgeDomain enables the configuration and management of Layer 2 virtual networks. It includes settings for VNI, EVI, route targets for import and export, and tunnel index allocation. Additionally, the specification allows for advanced features such as MAC address table limits, aging, Proxy ARP and detection of MAC and IP duplication.

  • configuredName string #

    The name of the BridgeDomain to configure on the device.

  • description string #

    The description of the BridgeDomain.

  • encapOptions object #

    Encapsulation-specific options (such as MPLS tunneling options).

    default: {"vxlan": {"tunnelIndexPool": "tunnel-index-pool", "vniPool": "vni-pool"}}
    • mpls object #

      MPLS encapsulation options. Only applicable if MPLS encapsulation is used (Type=EVPNMPLS).

      • allowedTunnelTypes array[string] #

        Allowed tunnel types for MPLS transport.

      • controlWord boolean #

        Enable or disable the use of control word. Applicable only to EVPNMPLS service Type.

    • vxlan object #

      VXLAN encapsulation options. Only applicable if VXLAN encapsulation is used (Type=EVPNVXLAN).

      • tunnelIndexPool string #

        Reference to a tunnel index pool to use for allocations.

        default: "tunnel-index-pool"
      • vni integer #

        VNI to use for this BridgeDomain, can be optionally left blank to have it allocated using the VNI Pool.

        range: 1 to 16777215
      • vniPool string #

        Reference to a VNI pool to use for allocations if VNI is left blank.

        default: "vni-pool"
  • evi integer #

    EVI to use for this BridgeDomain, can be optionally left blank to have it automatically allocated using the EVI Pool.

    range: 1 to 65535
  • eviPool string #

    Reference to an EVI pool to use for allocations if EVI is left blank.

    default: "evi-pool"
  • exportTarget string #

    Export route target in 'target:N:N' format, if not specified, the default value taken as "target:1:<evi>".

  • importTarget string #

    Import route target in 'target:N:N' format, if not specified, the default value taken as "target:1:<evi>".

  • l2proxyARPND object #

    Enables / Disabled Proxy ARP / Proxy ND.

    • dynamicLearning object #

      Configures dynamic learning of ARP/ND entries in the proxy table.

      • agingTimeSeconds integer #

        Aging timer value for the proxy entries in seconds. If not set, this indicates that the entries are never flushed.

        format: int32range: 60 to 86400
      • enabled boolean #

        Enables or disables Dynamic Learning.

        default: false
      • refreshIntervalSeconds integer #

        The interval determines the frequency at which the system generates three ARP Requests or Neighbor Solicitations with the intent to refresh the proxy entry. The refresh is sent within the age-time window.

        range: 120 to 86400
    • ipDuplication object #

      Configures IP duplication detection and resolution mechanisms.

      • enabled boolean #

        Enables or disables IP Duplication.

        default: false
      • holdDownTimeMinutes integer #

        Time to wait in minutes from the moment an IP is declared duplicate to the time the IP is removed from the proxy ARP/ND table.

        default: 9range: 2 to 60
      • monitoringWindowMinutes integer #

        Monitoring window for detecting duplication on a given IP address in the proxy ARP/ND table.

        default: 3range: 1 to 15
      • numMoves integer #

        Number of moves in the proxy ARP/ND table that an IP is allowed within the monitoring-window.

        default: 5range: 3 to 10
    • maxEntries integer #

      Maximum number of entries allowed in the proxy table of the bridge domain.

      default: 250range: 1 to 8192
    • proxyARP boolean #

      Enables proxy ARP.

      default: false
    • proxyND boolean #

      Enables proxy ND.

      default: false
  • macDuplicationDetection object #

    Enable or disable MAC duplication detection and resolution mechanisms.

    • action string #

      Action to take on the subinterface upon detecting at least one mac addresses as duplicate on the subinterface.

      default: "StopLearning"enum: "Blackhole", "OperDown", "StopLearning"
    • enabled boolean #

      Enables or disables Mac Duplication Detection.

      default: false
    • holdDownTimeMinutes integer #

      Time to wait in minutes from the moment a mac is declared duplicate to the mac is flushed from the bridge table.

      default: 9format: int32range: 2 to 60
    • monitoringWindowMinutes integer #

      Monitoring window in minutes for detecting duplication on a given mac address.

      default: 3format: int32range: 1 to 15
    • numMoves integer #

      Number of moves a mac is allowed within the monitoring-window, before it is declared duplicate.

      default: 5range: >= 3
  • macLearning object #

    Enable MAC learning for this BridgeDomain.

    default: {"agingTimeSeconds": 300, "enabled": true}
    • agingTimeSeconds integer #

      Configurable aging time for dynamically learned mac addresses.

      default: 300format: int32range: 60 to 86400
    • enabled* boolean #

      Enables or disables MAC learning for the BridgeDomain.

      default: true
  • macLimit integer #

    Sets the maximum number of MAC entries accepted in the bridge table.

    format: int32range: >= 1
  • type string #

    Select the type of BridgeDomain. Simple doesn't include any overlay control plane or dataplane properties (EVPN/VXLAN). EVPNVXLAN includes the properties needed to provision this BridgeDomain over an IP Fabric.

    default: "EVPNVXLAN"enum: "Simple", "EVPNVXLAN", "EVPNMPLS"

STATUS

BridgeDomainStatus defines the observed state of BridgeDomain

  • evi integer #

    EVI in use for this bridge domain.

  • exportTarget string #

    Export route target for this bridge domain.

  • health integer #

    Indicates the health score of the VNET.

  • healthScoreReason string #

    Indicates the reason for the health score.

  • importTarget string #

    Import route target for this bridge domain.

  • lastChangeTime string #

    The time when the state of the resource last changed.

  • nodes array[string] #

    Nodes which have the BridgeDomain configured (min 1 sub-interface).

  • numNodes integer #

    Number of nodes which have the BridgeDomain configured (min 1 sub-interface).

  • numSubinterfaces integer #

    Number of sub-interfaces attached to the BridgeDomain.

    format: int32
  • numSubinterfacesOperDown integer #

    Number of oper-down sub-interfaces attached to the BridgeDomain.

    format: int32
  • operationalState string #

    Operational state of the VNET.

    enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"
  • tunnelIndex integer #

    Vxlan tunnel index in use.

  • vni integer #

    VNI in use for this bridge domain.

services.eda.nokia.com / v2

SPEC

BridgeDomainInterconnectSpec defines the desired state of BridgeDomainInterconnect

  • bridgeDomainRef* object #

    Reference to the BridgeDomain instance to interconnect.

    • name* string #

      Name of the BridgeDomain instance.

    • namespace string #

      Namespace of the BridgeDomain instance, if different from the referencing BridgeDomainInterconnect namespace.

      default: "EVPN"enum: "EVPN"
    • encapsulation* string #

    • evi integer #

      Required for EVPN control plane on SRLinux

      format: int32
    • exportPolicy string #

      Reference to a Policy CR that will be used to export routes.

    • exportTarget string #

      Export route target in 'target:N:N' format. One of ExportTarget or ExportPolicy must be specified.

    • importPolicy string #

      Reference to a Policy CR that will be used to import routes.

    • importTarget string #

      Import route target in 'target:N:N' format. One of ImportTarget or ImportPolicy must be specified.

    • mpls object #

      MPLS encapsulation options. Only applicable if MPLS encapsulation is used.

      • allowedTunnelTypes array[string] #

        Allowed tunnel types for MPLS transport.

      • controlWord boolean #

        Enable or disable the use of control word. Applicable only to EVPNMPLS service Type.

    • vxlan object #

      VXLAN encapsulation options. Only applicable if VXLAN encapsulation is used.

      • tunnelIndexPool string #

        Reference to tunnel index allocation pool.

        default: "tunnel-index-pool"
      • vni integer #

        VNI for the service instance.

        format: int32range: 1 to 16777215
  • nodeSelectors array[string] #

    Label selector to select nodes on which to configure the BridgeDomainInterconnect.

  • nodes array[string] #

    List of nodes (gateways) on which to configure the BridgeDomainInterconnect.

STATUS

BridgeDomainInterconnectStatus defines the observed state of BridgeDomainInterconnect

  • health integer #

    Indicates the health score of the BridgeDomainInterconnect.

  • healthScoreReason string #

    Explains the reason for the health score.

  • lastChangeTime string #

    Timestamp of the last state change.

  • nodes array[string] #

    List of nodes on which the BridgeDomainInterconnect is deployed.

  • operationalState string #

    Operational state of the BridgeDomainInterconnect.

    enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"

BridgeInterface

services.eda.nokia.com / v2

Expand All

SPEC

The BridgeInterface enables the attachment of network interfaces to a Bridge Domain. It includes settings for VLAN ID allocation, interface attachment, and actions on ingress and egress traffic. The specification supports integration with other network resources, such as Bridge Domains and Interfaces, and provides advanced features like MAC Duplication Detection with configurable actions.

  • bridgeDomain* string #

    Reference to a BridgeDomain in which to attach the BridgeInterface.

  • description string #

    The description of the BridgeInterface.

  • egress object #

    Manages actions on traffic at Egress.

    • filters array[string] #

      List of Filter references to use at egress.

    • qosPolicies array[string] #

      List of QoS Egress policy references to use at egress.

  • ingress object #

    Manages actions on traffic at Ingress.

    • filters array[string] #

      List of Filter references to use at ingress.

    • qosPolicies array[string] #

      List of QoS Ingress policy references to use at ingress.

  • interface* string #

    Reference to an Interface resource to attach this BridgeInterface.

  • l2MTU integer #

    L2 MTU specifies the maximum sized Ethernet frame that can be transmitted on the subinterface. If a frame exceeds this size it is discarded. If the l2-mtu of the subinterface exceeds the port-mtu of the associated interface, the subinterface will remain operationally down.

    range: 1450 to 9500
  • macDuplicationDetectionAction string #

    Override for Mac Duplication Detection action if enabled in the associated BridgeDomain.

    enum: "Blackhole", "OperDown", "StopLearning", "BridgeDomainAction"
  • splitHorizonGroup string #

    Split Horizon Group to be used for this BridgeInterface. The subinterface within this BridgeInterface will be a member of this Split Horizon Group.

  • uplink object #

    The Uplink between your access breakout switch and your leaf switch.

    • egress object #

      Manages actions on traffic at Egress of the Local endpoint of the Uplink.

      • filters array[string] #

        List of Filter references to use at egress.

      • qosPolicies array[string] #

        List of QoS Egress policy references to use at egress.

    • ingress object #

      Manages actions on traffic at Ingress of the Local endpoint of the Uplink.

      • filters array[string] #

        List of Filter references to use at ingress.

      • qosPolicies array[string] #

        List of QoS Ingress policy references to use at ingress.

    • uplinkSelectors array[string] #

      Selects TopoLinks which connect a leaf switch to a breakout switch. This is the uplink between your access breakout switch and your leaf switch. There can only be a single TopoLink between the access breakout switch and the leaf switch, if more than one TopoLink is present between two devices the transaction will fail.

    • uplinkVLANID string #

      The VLAN ID to be utilized to isolate traffic from the VLAN on the access breakout switch to the leaf switch on the selected uplink TopoLink.

      default: "pool"
    • uplinkVLANPool string #

      A VLAN from this pool will be utilized to isolate traffic from the VLAN on the access breakout switch to the leaf switch on the selected uplink TopoLink.

  • vlanID* string #

    Single value between 1-4094 support, ranges supported in the format x-y,x-y, or the special keyword null, any, untagged or pool for auto allocation.

STATUS

  • health integer #

    Indicates the health score of the BridgeInterface.

  • healthScoreReason string #

    Indicates the reason for the health score.

  • lastChangeTime string #

    The time when the state of the resource last changed.

  • operationalState string #

    Operational state of the BridgeInterface.

    enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"
  • subinterfaces array[object] #

    Sub-interfaces status within the BridgeInterface.

    • [] object #
      • enabled boolean #

        The administrative status of the SubInterface.

      • interface* string #

        Normalized interface name.

      • interfaceResource* string #

        Eda interface resource.

      • lastChangeTime string #

        Indicates when this SubInterface last changed state.

      • node* string #

        Reference to Node object.

      • nodeInterface* string #

        Node specific interface name.

      • operatingSystem string #

        Operating System of the Node.

      • operationalState string #

        Indicates the current operational state of the SubInterface.

        enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"
      • subinterfaceIndex integer #

        Index allocated to the subinterface on the associated interface for the given VLAN.

      • vlanID string #

        vlan assigned to this subinterface.

  • uplinks array[object] #

    Uplinks status within the BridgeInterface.

    • [] object #
      • enabled boolean #

        The administrative status of the SubInterface.

      • interface* string #

        Normalized interface name.

      • interfaceResource* string #

        Eda interface resource.

      • lastChangeTime string #

        Indicates when this SubInterface last changed state.

      • node* string #

        Reference to Node object.

      • nodeInterface* string #

        Node specific interface name.

      • operatingSystem string #

        Operating System of the Node.

      • operationalState string #

        Indicates the current operational state of the SubInterface.

        enum: "Up", "Down", "Degraded", "Unknown"
      • subinterfaceIndex integer #

        Index allocated to the subinterface on the associated interface for the given VLAN.

      • vlanID string #

        vlan assigned to this subinterface.

docs.title: DHCP Relay
docs.text:
DHCP → DRDHCP Relays

Documentation coming soon!

docs.title: Custom Resource Definition
docs.text:
To browse the Custom Resource Definition go to crd.eda.dev.

DHCPRelay

services.eda.nokia.com / v2

Expand All

SPEC

The DHCPRelay enables the forwarding of DHCP requests and responses between clients and servers across different networks. This resource allows for the configuration of various DHCP relay sub-options, such as CircuitID, RemoteID, and ClientLinkLayerAddress, to provide detailed client information. It also includes settings for specifying the router to reach the DHCP server, the list of DHCP servers to forward requests to, and selectors for Routed and IRB interfaces where the relay will be configured. Additionally, the GI Address option can be set to derive the Gateway IP address from the selected interface, ensuring correct routing of DHCP messages.

  • giAddress boolean #

    Set GI Address to the IP derived from the IRBInterface or RoutedInterface on which the DHCP relay is configured.

  • irbInterfaceSelectors array[string] #

    Label selector to select the IRBInterface on which to configure the DHCP relay.

  • routedInterfaceSelectors array[string] #

    Label selector to select the RoutedInterface on which to configure the DHCP relay.

  • router string #

    Router to be used to reach the DHCP server, if not specified the Router under which the source IRBInterface or RoutedInterface resides will be used.

  • servers* array[string] #

    List of servers to send the DHCP relayed packet to. These can be IP addresses or FQDN.

  • subOptions array[string] #

    DHCP Relay sub-options; available options are CircuitID, RemoteID, and ClientLinkLayerAddress.

STATUS

DHCPRelayStatus defines the observed state of DHCPRelay
